summary: English 18th-century portraitist, first president of the Royal Academy of Arts. Most commonly identified by Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse. Articulated the "Grand Style" / "Grand Manner" in his Discourses on Art. Rival of Thomas Gainsborough. Co-founder of the Royal Academy with Angelica Kauffman and others.

works.name: Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse (1784)
works.description: His most famous painting. Depicts the actress Sarah Siddons enthroned as Melpomene, the muse of tragedy, with allegorical figures of Pity and Terror behind her. Two versions exist (Huntington and Dulwich). Wikipedia

images.caption: Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse (1784)

clues.clue: Depicts Sarah Siddons seated on a throne as Melpomene, the muse of tragedy
examples: this artist depicted an English actress sitting on a throne playing the role of Melpomene
an actress sitting on a throne in his portrait Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse

clues.clue: Allegorical figures of Pity and Terror stand behind her (holding a cup/chalice and a dagger)
examples: the allegorical figures of Pity and Terror behind a throne
A pale woman sits in front of allegories of Pity and Terror, respectively holding a cup and dagger

clues.clue: Siddons wears a string of pearls, a diamond diadem, and a gold/brown dress with scalloped leaves
examples: A woman wears a string of pearls, a diamond diadem, and a gold dress with scalloped leaves in a portrait of a Welsh tragedienne

clues.clue: Reynolds used himself to model the facial expression of the figure allegorizing Pity
examples: He used himself to model the facial expressions of the figure allegorizing Pity

clues.clue: Two versions exist: Dulwich and Huntington; original dress was blue, not yellow
examples: A study on the differences between the Dulwich and Huntington versions revealed that the title figure's dress was originally blue rather than yellow

clues.clue: Gainsborough reportedly exclaimed about Siddons's nose 'There's no end to it!' while painting her
examples: Thomas Gainsborough reportedly exclaimed about this woman's nose, 'There's no end to it!'

clues.clue: The award statuette in the film All About Eve is modeled after this painting; called the 'Sarah Siddons Award'
examples: Addison DeWitt narrates a scene in which this object, which is modeled after the Joshua Reynolds painting Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse, is given to the title character

works.name: Colonel Acland and Lord Sydney: The Archers (1769)
works.description: A life-sized double portrait of two aristocrats hunting with bows and arrows in a forest. Acland wears green and Sydney yellow. Purchased by the Tate in 2005. Wikipedia

images.caption: Colonel Acland and Lord Sydney: The Archers (1769)

clues.clue: Two aristocrats drawing/stringing bows in a forested area, with dead game behind them
examples: two aristocrats taking aim with bows and arrows in a forest
two men leave trails of dead game in their wake as they draw their bows

clues.clue: Acland wears green and Sydney wears yellow (contrasting colors)
examples: Colonel Acland and Lord Sydney wearing contrasting colors of green and yellow while preparing to fire their bows

works.name: Portrait of Augustus Keppel (1752-53)
works.description: Portrait of the naval officer striking the pose of the Apollo Belvedere against a stormy seascape. Established Reynolds's reputation. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Keppel is posed in the reverse pose of the Apollo Belvedere, on a crag against a stormy sea
examples: Augustus Keppel takes the pose of Apollo Belvedere
This artist reversed the pose of the Apollo Belvedere in a painting set against the backdrop of a stormy sea

clues.clue: A later portrait shows Keppel resting his hand on a sword, representing order restored after court-martial
examples: He represented order being restored to a court-martialed navy officer by having Lord Keppel rest his hand upon a sword

works.name: David Garrick Between Tragedy and Comedy (1761)
works.description: Depicts the actor David Garrick caught between the muses of Comedy and Tragedy. Parodies the classical story of Hercules choosing between virtue and pleasure. Wikipedia

images.caption: David Garrick Between Tragedy and Comedy (1761)

clues.clue: Parodies the classical story of Hercules choosing between virtue and pleasure; Garrick looks at a blue-robed woman (Tragedy)
examples: This painter parodied the classical story of Hercules picking between virtue and pleasure in his depiction of a famous actor looking at a blue robed woman
This advocate of the Grand Style painted David Garrick between Comedy and Tragedy

works.name: Discourses on Art / Grand Style
works.description: Reynolds's lecture series delivered to Royal Academy students, articulating his vision of the "Grand Style" — idealizing subjects, using visual metaphor and symbolism for noble virtues, following Renaissance art.

clues.clue: Articulated the 'Grand Style' / 'Grand Manner' in his Discourses on Art
examples: this artist laid out his idea of a 'grand style' in a series of lectures titled the Discourses on Art
argued against copying subjects exactly as they appear in nature as part of his 'Grand Style'

clues.clue: Grand Style: emphasizes idealizing subjects, erasing faults, generalizing to ideal forms
examples: Reynolds suggested that portraitists should paint their subjects in an idealized manner that erases their faults
It emphasizes generalizing to ideal forms, using visual metaphor and overt symbolism for noble virtues

clues.clue: Argued painters should not portray the defects of Alexander the Great or Agesilaus
examples: He argued that painters should not portray the defects of Alexander the Great or Agesilaus in a lecture series on history painting

works.name: The Age of Innocence (c. 1788)
works.description: Character study of a young girl in a yellow dress. Edith Wharton took the title of her novel from this painting. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Character study of a young girl in a yellow dress; Edith Wharton took her novel title from this painting
examples: his image of a girl in a yellow dress that lends its title to Edith Wharton novel The Age of Innocence
This artist produced an oft-printed character study of a young girl, from which Edith Wharton took her title for The Age of Innocence

works.name: The Ladies Waldegrave
works.description: Triple portrait of three grandnieces of Horace Walpole, all in identical white gowns and gray powdered wigs, embroidering with silk around a table — an allusion to the Fates. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Three grandnieces of Horace Walpole in identical white gowns and powdered gray wigs, embroidering silk around a table — allusion to the Fates
examples: This triple portrait shows the grandnieces of Horace Walpole embroidering with silk around a small table, in a likely allusion to the Fates
painted three sisters in white dresses and gray wigs sitting around a table in a work commissioned by their mother in the hope of attracting suitors

works.name: Portrait of Omai (Mai)
works.description: Portrait of a Polynesian man (Mai, from the Society Islands) standing in the adlocutio (orator's) pose in white robes. Jointly purchased by the National Portrait Gallery and the Getty Museum in April 2023. Called 'Britain's first grand portrayal of a non-white subject.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Polynesian man (Omai/Mai) in the adlocutio pose; jointly purchased by the National Portrait Gallery and the Getty Museum (April 2023); called 'Britain's first grand portrayal of a non-white subject'
examples: This Reynolds portrait of a Polynesian man standing in the adlocutio pose was jointly purchased by the National Portrait Gallery and the Getty Museum in April 2023. According to The Guardian, this painting is Britain's 'first grand portrayal of a non-white subject.'

comprehensive_summary:
Joshua Reynolds (1723–1792) was an 18th-century English portraitist and the first president of the Royal Academy of Arts, co-founded with Angelica Kauffman, Mary Moser, and others. He is most commonly identified in quizbowl by his painting Sarah Siddons as the Tragic Muse (1784), which depicts the actress Sarah Siddons seated on a throne as Melpomene, the muse of tragedy, with allegorical figures of Pity (holding a cup) and Terror (holding a dagger) behind her. Siddons wears pearls, a diadem, and a gold dress with scalloped leaves. Reynolds used himself to model the facial expression of Pity. Two versions exist (Dulwich and Huntington), and the original dress was blue, not yellow. Gainsborough reportedly exclaimed about Siddons's nose 'There's no end to it!' while painting his own portrait of her. The award statuette in the film All About Eve is modeled after this painting (called the 'Sarah Siddons Award').

His other major works include: Colonel Acland and Lord Sydney: The Archers (1769), two aristocrats in contrasting green and yellow hunting with bows and arrows in a forest; Portrait of Augustus Keppel (1752–53), reversing the pose of the Apollo Belvedere on a crag against a stormy sea — this portrait established Reynolds's reputation; David Garrick Between Tragedy and Comedy (1761), parodying Hercules choosing between virtue and pleasure; The Age of Innocence, a character study of a young girl in a yellow dress that gave Edith Wharton her novel title; The Ladies Waldegrave, a triple portrait of three grandnieces of Horace Walpole in identical white gowns and gray wigs embroidering silk — an allusion to the Fates; and Portrait of Omai (Mai), a Polynesian man in the adlocutio pose, jointly purchased by the National Portrait Gallery and Getty Museum in April 2023 and called 'Britain's first grand portrayal of a non-white subject.'

Other noted works include: Master Crewe as Henry VIII (child posed as the Holbein portrait); Portrait of Giuseppe Baretti (myopic critic holding a book to his nose); Lady Sarah Bunbury Sacrificing to the Graces; Infant Academy; and Mrs. Lloyd, whose subject is the Reynolds portrait that Lily Bart reenacts in a tableaux vivants scene in Edith Wharton's The House of Mirth. Henry James's novel The Outcry features a plot to sell a Reynolds painting to an American billionaire.

Reynolds articulated the 'Grand Style' or 'Grand Manner' in his Discourses on Art, emphasizing idealizing subjects, erasing faults, generalizing to ideal forms, and following Renaissance models. He argued painters should not portray defects of Alexander the Great or Agesilaus. The Pre-Raphaelites mocked him as 'Sir Sloshua' and sought to return art to a style before his influence. He insisted Benjamin West paint the Death of General Wolfe figures in togas (West ignored him). Reynolds described Velazquez's Portrait of Innocent X as 'the finest picture in Rome.'

He was a famous rival of Thomas Gainsborough; a myth claims that Reynolds's Eighth Discourse on color theory prompted Gainsborough to paint The Blue Boy. Reynolds praised Gainsborough's Girl with Pigs but called his late works 'fancy pictures.' Reynolds was close friends with Angelica Kauffman — he helped her escape a fraudulent marriage and they exchanged portraits — but Nathaniel Hone satirized their friendship in The Conjurer. In Johann Zoffany's group portrait of Royal Academy members, the two female founders (Kauffman and Moser) appear only as portraits on the wall, since propriety prevented their presence with nude male models.
